sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Summary Rules

symptom: Summary Rule create/update returns HTTP 400 'User {principalId} does not maintain access to action workspaces/tables/write at scope {workspace}'
rootCause: User creating/modifying the summary rule lacks Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/tables/write permission needed to create/modify the destination table
solution: Grant the user Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/tables/write permission at workspace scope via Azure RBAC role assignment
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Summary Rules

symptom: Cannot create new summary rules that use 'union *' or 'isfuzzy=true' operators in the query. Existing rules with these operators continue to work
rootCause: Microsoft blocked usage of union * and isfuzzy=true in new summary rules creation due to a security concern (since Nov 2025)
solution: Rewrite the summary rule query to avoid union * and isfuzzy=true. Specify explicit table names instead. Existing rules are unaffected. See implementation considerations in public docs
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Summary Rules

symptom: Cannot change ContainerLog table plan from 'Basic' to 'Analytics' via the Azure Portal UI
rootCause: Known UI bug in Azure Portal (ADO work item #53588). The portal does not properly support this plan change for ContainerLog table
solution: Use Azure CLI (az monitor log-analytics workspace table update) or REST API to change the ContainerLog table plan instead of the portal UI
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Table management/Basic and Auxiliary table plans

symptom: Cannot change table plan from 'Auxiliary' to 'Analytics' in Log Analytics workspace
rootCause: By design - once a table is created with Auxiliary plan, the plan cannot be switched to Analytics. This is a permanent, irreversible configuration
solution: This is expected behavior per documentation. Plan ahead before creating Auxiliary tables. If Analytics plan needed, create a new table with Analytics plan and migrate data
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Table management/Basic and Auxiliary table plans

symptom: AzureDiagnostics table does not support Basic Logs plan. If Azure portal allows changing AzureDiagnostics to Basic, it is a regression bug
rootCause: By design - AzureDiagnostics is not supported for Basic Logs. Microsoft plans to move RPs to dedicated resource-specific tables where Basic plan can be enabled by RP owners
solution: Cannot set AzureDiagnostics to Basic. Recommend migrating to resource-specific destination tables. If portal allows changing AzureDiagnostics plan, report as regression bug to PG (previously occurred Dec 2022)
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Table management/Basic and Auxiliary table plans

symptom: Cannot purge data from Log Analytics using regex, contains, or other unsupported operators via the Purge API. Only ==, =~, in, in~, >, >=, <, <=, between are supported
rootCause: Log Analytics Purge API has a limited set of supported filter operators by design. Complex pattern matching operators like regex and contains are not supported
solution: Workaround using _ItemId column: 1) Run KQL query with the unsupported operator to identify target records 2) Project only _ItemId from results 3) Use Purge API with 'in' operator on _ItemId column passing collected IDs. This enables purging by any KQL filter criteria
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Table management/How to: Overcome Limitations in Log Analytics Purge API by Using the ItemId Column

symptom: Under Azure Monitor Private Link Scope (AMPLS), Azure Monitor Resources shows 'Failed' provisioning state after connecting target components. Re-adding doesn't help. Activity logs show private endpoint connection error.
rootCause: Known bug on private endpoint connection causing scoped resources to get stuck in 'Failed' provisioning state
solution: 1) Navigate to the Private Endpoint resource associated with the AMPLS (via 'Private Endpoint connections' blade) 2) On the Private Endpoint Overview blade, follow the yellow warning banner instruction to click Refresh 3) Navigate back to AMPLS and confirm all scoped resources show 'Succeeded' state (click Refresh if needed)
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Known Issues/AMPLS/Azure monitor private link scope: Azure Monitor Resources shows 'Failed' provisioning state

symptom: Cannot create new Log Analytics workspaces or move existing ones to the 'Free' tier since July 1st 2022
rootCause: By design: the legacy Free Trial pricing tier has been deprecated and disabled since July 2022. Workspaces already on Free Trial can remain.
solution: Inform customer this is by design. Point to public documentation: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-monitor/logs/cost-logs#free-trial-pricing-tier. Existing Free Trial workspaces can stay on that tier.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Known Issues/Billing/By design: Cannot create new workspaces or move existing ones to the 'Free' tier since July 1st 2022

symptom: parse_json() or extend/project returns error 'Query could not be parsed at .' when parsing JSON properties that are KQL reserved keywords (e.g. 'title')
rootCause: KQL reserved keywords (like 'title') cannot be used directly as property accessors after parse_json() - the parser interprets them as language tokens rather than property names
solution: Use identifier quoting with bracket notation: parse_json(json).['title'] or parse_json(json).["title"]. Alternatively rename the property to a non-reserved keyword (e.g. 'Title' with capital T). Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-explorer/kusto/query/schema-entities/entity-names#identifier-quoting
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Known Issues/Query experience/parse_json() may return error "Query could not be parsed at '.'" once you parse special items

symptom: Azure Data Factory Analytics summary tile stuck in 'Performing Assessment' even though ADF data is present in the Log Analytics workspace
rootCause: The ADF Product Group confirmed the Summary box feature has been deprecated
solution: Direct customers to use the 'AzureDataFactoryAnalytics' workbook instead of the deprecated summary tile. Close the support case under ADF root cause tree. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-factory/monitor-using-azure-monitor#monitor-data-factory-metrics
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Known Issues/Solutions/Azure Data Factory Analytics summary tile stuck in "Performing Assessment"

symptom: Data exported from Log Analytics to Event Hub appears to show fewer records than workspace - Event Hub metrics show same number for both 'Incoming Requests' and 'Incoming Messages'
rootCause: Data Export sends records in batches to Event Hub. A bug in Event Hub causes it to report the number of batches for BOTH 'Incoming Requests' and 'Incoming Messages' metrics, instead of showing total records for Messages.
solution: Use Kusto query on EventHubSendBatchTelemetry (cluster: azureinsights) to verify actual record count: query shows TotalRecords (sum of numberOfRecords) vs Batches (count). Requests = batches sent, Messages should = total records but currently shows batches too due to EH bug.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data export/Did all my LA data make it to Event Hub using Data Export?

symptom: Data export rule includes unsupported table - configuration succeeds but no data is exported for that table
rootCause: Unsupported tables can be included in data export rule configuration without error, but the export process silently skips them
solution: Verify the table is in the supported tables list: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-monitor/logs/logs-data-export?tabs=portal#supported-tables. If unsupported, remove it from the export rule. Data export will commence automatically if the table becomes supported later.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data export/Log Analytics workspace Data Export  - Troubleshooting

symptom: Data export rule fails with error 'Table does not exist in the workspace'
rootCause: The data export rule includes a table name that does not exist in the Log Analytics workspace
solution: Verify the table name is correct and exists in the workspace. Remove or correct the non-existent table from the data export rule.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data export/Log Analytics workspace Data Export  - Troubleshooting

symptom: TimeGenerated values differ between Log Analytics workspace and exported data (Storage/Event Hub) - exported records show original TimeGenerated while workspace shows adjusted values
rootCause: LA ingestion pipeline adjusts TimeGenerated if the value is older than 2 days before received time or more than 1 day in the future. These adjustments are NOT reflected in exported data.
solution: This is by design. TimeGenerated adjustments made during ingestion are not applied to exported data. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-monitor/logs/log-standard-columns#timegenerated. See wiki article 'TimeGenerated adjustments on Data Export' for workaround.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data export/Log Analytics workspace Data Export  - Troubleshooting

symptom: Data export to Event Hub stopped working unexpectedly without customer making any changes to the data export rule
rootCause: Product team may blocklist the workspace if the Event Hub destination is incorrectly configured and causing severe backlog impacting other customers
solution: 1) Check if workspace is blocklisted: open Tenants-Prod.json in EngSys-MDA-OBO repo (or sovereign cloud equivalent) 2) Search by workspace ID for 'Blocked.FirstTags' config 3) If blocklisted, raise IcM to 'Azure Monitor Essentials Sev3 CRI - Diagnostic Logs on-call' or reach out to LA SME
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data export/Log Analytics workspace Data Export  - Troubleshooting

symptom: Export to storage fails with 'StorageException' error - PT1H.json files written with 0 bytes to Azure Storage blobs by diagnostic settings
rootCause: Immutable policy configured on the destination storage account conflicts with the data export write operations
solution: Review the immutable policy on the destination storage account. Ensure the storage account blob immutability policy allows append operations required by data export. See Known Issue 10952 for details.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data export/Log Analytics workspace Data Export  - Troubleshooting

symptom: BadRequest (400) when trying to create a Data Collection Rule (DCR) for Event Hub data source ingestion to Log Analytics
rootCause: The target Log Analytics workspace is not linked to a dedicated cluster or does not have a CapacityReservation SKU. Event Hub data imports require LACluster or CapacityReservation SKU.
solution: Link the workspace to a dedicated cluster or set the workspace SKU to CapacityReservation before creating the DCR for Event Hub ingestion.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data sources/APIs/Ingest events from Azure Event Hub to Logs

symptom: Data stops flowing from Event Hub to Log Analytics after customer moves subscription to a different tenant
rootCause: The DCR Managed Service Identity (MSI) and RBAC permissions become invalid after a cross-tenant subscription transfer.
solution: Customer needs to recreate the DCR MSI and reassign the permissions (Azure Event Hubs Data Receiver or Data Owner role) to the event hub.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data sources/APIs/Ingest events from Azure Event Hub to Logs

symptom: Event Hub data ingestion to Log Analytics: data flows but lands only in TimeGenerated and RawData columns instead of the intended custom table columns
rootCause: The DCR does not include a transformKql to extract and map Event Hub JSON fields to custom table columns. Without transformation, all data goes to the RawData column by default.
solution: Update the DCR to include a transformKql that parses the incoming JSON and maps fields to the appropriate columns in the destination table.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data sources/APIs/Ingest events from Azure Event Hub to Logs

symptom: DCRA creation for Event Hub to Log Analytics fails or data does not flow after creating Event Hub DCR
rootCause: The DCR managed identity (system or user-assigned) has not been granted RBAC permissions (Azure Event Hubs Data Receiver or Azure Event Hubs Data Owner) on the event hub or event hub namespace.
solution: Go to the event hub or event hub namespace, grant Azure Event Hubs Data Receiver or Azure Event Hubs Data Owner role to the MSI configured in the DCR.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data sources/APIs/Ingest events from Azure Event Hub to Logs

symptom: No AzureActivity data coming from one or more subscriptions (legacy mode). Operation table shows error: Access to the subscription was lost
rootCause: The subscription was transferred to another Azure Active Directory tenant, breaking the Azure Activity log connection to the Log Analytics workspace.
solution: Follow the steps in HT: Reconnect a subscription to Azure Activity log data source. Also recommend migrating from the deprecated legacy mode to the diagnostic settings mode.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data sources/Azure Activity Logs/TSG: No AzureActivity data coming from one or more subscriptions

symptom: Custom Logs V2 (Log Ingestion API): data accepted with HTTP 200/204 but ProcessedChunk shows code NoExpectedStartArray and data does not appear in destination table
rootCause: The body of the API call is not a JSON array. The Logs Ingestion API requires the body to be a JSON array, even for single-item payloads.
solution: Ensure the request body is a valid JSON array (e.g., [{...}] for a single item).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data sources/APIs/Custom Logs V2: Send Custom Logs(v2) using Log Ingestion API

symptom: Custom Logs V2 (Log Ingestion API): data accepted with HTTP 200/204 but ProcessedChunk shows code Filtered:NoLogAnalyticsDestination and data is missing from destination table
rootCause: The configured custom table does not exist on the target Log Analytics workspace at the time of ingestion request.
solution: Verify that the custom table exists on the target Log Analytics workspace. If recently created, allow up to 5 minutes for configuration propagation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data sources/APIs/Custom Logs V2: Send Custom Logs(v2) using Log Ingestion API

symptom: Custom Logs V2 (Log Ingestion API): data accepted but TimeGenerated column has incorrect or missing values. LogTrace shows ProcessedDataTimeInfo with missing value.
rootCause: The TimeGenerated field was not properly populated - either not specified in a supported datetime format or not provided at all.
solution: Ensure the TimeGenerated field value is in a supported datetime format as documented at: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/data-explorer/kusto/query/scalar-data-types/datetime#supported-formats
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data sources/APIs/Custom Logs V2: Send Custom Logs(v2) using Log Ingestion API

symptom: HTTP Data Collector API returns 400 Bad Request with Error=InvalidDataFormat when sending custom logs
rootCause: Common causes: (1) using a reserved field name in the payload (e.g., Tenant, TimeGenerated), (2) invalid JSON format (missing commas/brackets), or (3) invalid data values (e.g., unquoted datetime).
solution: (1) Use different/unique field names avoiding reserved names. (2) Validate JSON in VSCode. (3) Ensure datetime values are within quotation marks in proper format.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data sources/APIs/How to: Investigate HTTP Data Collector API Custom Logs

symptom: HTTP Data Collector API returns 403 Forbidden with Error=InvalidAuthorization: An invalid signature was specified in the Authorization header
rootCause: The HMAC signature generated by the customer code/script is invalid. Can also be caused by TLS inspection on proxies/firewalls between the data source and ODS endpoint.
solution: 1) Test with a known-good sample from public docs. If that works, revise custom code. 2) Verify no TLS inspection proxy is modifying the request.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data sources/APIs/How to: Investigate HTTP Data Collector API Custom Logs

symptom: HTTP Data Collector API returns 403 Forbidden with message about time difference exceeding 15 minutes between local system and Azure
rootCause: The local system clock sending the logs is not synchronized with Azure time. The maximum allowed time difference is 15 minutes.
solution: Adjust the local time of the machine(s) sending the custom logs to ensure clock synchronization with Azure (NTP sync recommended).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data sources/APIs/How to: Investigate HTTP Data Collector API Custom Logs

symptom: HTTP Data Collector API returns 403 Forbidden when workspace is scoped to Azure Monitor Private Link Scope (AMPLS) with public ingestion disabled
rootCause: The workspace has public data ingestion set to Disabled in AMPLS configuration. ODS drops ingestion calls from resources outside the VNet.
solution: Ensure the request is routed through the VNet associated with AMPLS, or enable public data ingestion on the workspace if appropriate.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data sources/APIs/How to: Investigate HTTP Data Collector API Custom Logs

symptom: HTTP Data Collector API returns 403 Forbidden when DisableLocalAuth is enabled on the Log Analytics workspace
rootCause: The DisableLocalAuth property is set to True on the workspace, which blocks authentication via workspace key (used by HTTP Data Collector API).
solution: Verify the DisableLocalAuth property value (needs to be null or False). If True, customer must update the flag per public documentation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data sources/APIs/How to: Investigate HTTP Data Collector API Custom Logs

symptom: HTTP Data Collector API: Operation table shows error Custom log is V2, Workspace cannot be modified when sending data to a custom log table
rootCause: The table was migrated to Custom Logs V2 schema (or created as CLV2), which locks the table from accepting new fields via the Data Collector API.
solution: Migrate the data ingestion from HTTP Data Collector API to the Log Ingestion API (Custom Logs V2).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data sources/APIs/How to: Investigate HTTP Data Collector API Custom Logs

symptom: HTTP Data Collector API: Operation table shows error Data of type was dropped: Custom log limit for workspace reached for customer
rootCause: The workspace has reached the maximum number (limit) of custom log tables allowed.
solution: Review and consolidate existing custom log tables. Remove unused tables or contact support if the limit needs to be increased.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Data sources/APIs/How to: Investigate HTTP Data Collector API Custom Logs

symptom: Duplicate records in Log Analytics workspace where _ItemId is the same across duplicates (verified via hash-based duplicate detection query)
rootCause: Ingestion pipeline re-ingested data from staging areas to prevent data loss during local instability, resulting in duplicate records with the same _ItemId.
solution: This is a known pipeline behavior. Escalate to Kusto-LogAnalytics Backend PG via standard CRI procedure if duplicate rate exceeds 3%. For customer workaround, use KQL deduplication.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Ingestion pipeline/TSG: Identify duplicate records and handle them

symptom: Duplicate records in Log Analytics workspace where _ItemId is different across duplicates (hash-based detection finds duplicates but _ItemId-based does not)
rootCause: The source/client is sending duplicate data to Log Analytics. Common cause: multiple diagnostic settings configured for the same resource sending to the same workspace.
solution: 1) Check for duplicate diagnostic settings on the source resource. 2) Contact TA/SME for further investigation. 3) If source is a Resource Provider, open collaboration with the RP team.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Ingestion pipeline/TSG: Identify duplicate records and handle them

symptom: Log Analytics Dedicated Cluster: cluster creation succeeds in portal GUI notification but cluster is not fully provisioned and workspaces cannot be linked
rootCause: The portal notification only indicates the cluster resource was created, not that provisioning is complete. Resource allocation can take several hours.
solution: Monitor the cluster ProvisioningState property using az CLI: az monitor log-analytics cluster show. Wait until ProvisioningState shows Succeeded before linking workspaces.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Dedicated Cluster/TSG: Dedicated Clusters

symptom: Customer reports truncated or trimmed data in Log Analytics workspace table fields, with trimming event messages in _LogOperation/Operation table showing field size exceeded Z bytes
rootCause: Input field from data source exceeds Log Analytics field size limit (32KB default for most tables including custom logs; 64KB for select Microsoft RP-owned tables that explicitly increased it)
solution: 1) Check Workspace Insights, Operation table, or _LogOperation function for trimming events identifying affected field and table 2) For custom log tables: customer must reduce field size below limit 3) For standard tables/AzureDiagnostics: transfer case to owning RP team 4) Use query AffectedTable | where string_size(X) >= Z to find affected records 5) If field name is a nesting input field, the source must reduce nested content size
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Ingestion pipeline/TSG: Investigate truncated, trimmed and multi-lined data in Log Analytics

symptom: Syslog messages in Log Analytics Syslog table appear truncated - data is cut off at the first colon character in the SyslogMessage field when using OMS agent
rootCause: OMS agent bug - colon character in syslog message is incorrectly detected as the start of the MESSAGE portion per RFC parsing, causing everything before the colon to be treated as header and truncated from SyslogMessage
solution: 1) Verify by running tcpdump on port 25224 on the agent machine and comparing captured message with Syslog table entry 2) Upgrade to AMA v1.14.5+ which supports Syslog RFC for network devices 3) For OMS agent, workaround is to use custom logs instead of Syslog table 4) This fix is NOT backported to OMS agent
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Ingestion pipeline/TSG: Investigate truncated, trimmed and multi-lined data in Log Analytics

symptom: ContainerLog LogEntry field values never exceed 16384 bytes and appear truncated; JSON payloads in LogEntry are not expandable in the Logs portal UI
rootCause: Docker logging engine hardcodes maximum 16KB (16384 bytes) per log entry line. When a log entry exceeds this limit, the remaining content is written to the next ContainerLog record. Each split record contains an incomplete JSON fragment, making it non-expandable. This is by design.
solution: 1) Enable ContainerLogV2 with multi-line logging support to resolve the splitting issue 2) If staying on ContainerLog old schema: explain this is Docker by-design behavior - consecutive LogEntry records are continuations of the same original log 3) Records with exactly 16384 bytes followed by a continuation record confirm this behavior
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Ingestion pipeline/TSG: Investigate truncated, trimmed and multi-lined data in Log Analytics

symptom: Data is not flowing or queryable from VNET after configuring Azure Monitor Private Link Scope (AMPLS) - endpoints not resolving to private IP addresses
rootCause: DNS not correctly configured for Private Link: Private DNS zone created without A records (overrides global endpoints with empty zone), missing Private DNS zone association to VNET, or Private Endpoint DNS settings incomplete
solution: 1) Verify VNET has required Private DNS zones attached with no NSGs/firewalls blocking 2) Check Private Endpoint DNS settings contain required A records 3) From VM in VNET, run nslookup on Azure Monitor endpoints to verify private IP resolution via privatelink CNAME 4) Correct order: create AMPLS scope first, create PE to scope, then create Private DNS zone with PE FQDNs 5) Use tcping <URL> 443 to verify connectivity
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Private link/Azure Monitor Private Link Scope

symptom: Cannot send telemetry data from on-premises applications through Azure Monitor Private Link endpoint - data not reaching Log Analytics workspace
rootCause: DNS forwarder not configured to forward DNS resolution requests from on-premises network to Azure Private DNS zone server, so on-prem machines resolve Azure Monitor endpoints to public IPs instead of private IPs
solution: Configure DNS forwarder per Azure Private Endpoint DNS documentation so that on-prem DNS requests are forwarded to Azure Private DNS zone and resolve to private IP addresses
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Private link/Azure Monitor Private Link Scope

symptom: Log queries fail with Access to resource is only permitted through an attached Private Link or Private link connection does not allow querying resource not attached to the current scope after AMPLS Network Isolation enforcement
rootCause: Network Isolation setting Allow public network access for queries is disabled and workspace is not connected to any AMPLS scope, or AMPLS QueryAccessMode is set to Private Only blocking queries to resources outside the scope. Since September 2021, Network Isolation is strictly enforced.
solution: 1) Check Network Isolation settings in ASC or Azure Portal 2) Either configure AMPLS with private endpoint for private query access, or change Network Isolation to allow public network queries 3) If AMPLS QueryAccessMode is Private Only, only resources within the scope can be queried from the private network
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Private link/Azure Monitor Private Link Scope

symptom: Queries using control commands (dot-commands like .show) fail when executed through ADX Proxy to Azure Monitor resources
rootCause: Control commands are generally not supported through the ADX Proxy. Only a small subset of .show commands related to viewing schema are supported to allow working with Kusto Explorer Client.
solution: 1) Run control commands directly against the ADX cluster, not through the proxy 2) For schema viewing, use only the supported .show commands 3) For query syntax, use Azure Monitor Query Language via the cross-resource format documented in official docs
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Query execution/ADX Proxy

symptom: Cross-tenant queries through ADX Proxy to Azure Monitor resources fail with authentication errors - AAD token scoped to single tenant
rootCause: The AAD token used to authenticate to ADX/Azure Monitor is scoped to a single tenant and cannot access resources in another tenant through the proxy
solution: Use the documented cross-tenant workaround: query data from each cluster/tenant separately and join results client-side, or use Azure Lighthouse for cross-tenant access
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Query execution/ADX Proxy

symptom: Queries from ADX cluster to Azure Monitor workspace via ADX Proxy fail when Private Link access is enforced on the Azure Monitor resource
rootCause: ADX Proxy queries originate from the ADX cluster which is outside the AMPLS private network. When Private Link enforcement is enabled, queries from non-private sources are blocked. Private link and IP restrictions are not supported for ADX Proxy.
solution: 1) Add the ADX cluster into the customers VNET to enable private access 2) Alternatively, query directly against the Azure Monitor resource and pull ADX data using the adx() pattern instead
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Query execution/ADX Proxy

symptom: Query is blocked with HTTP 400 and error code QueryCostError stating query was consuming too many resources and blocked for several minutes
rootCause: Query identified as consuming excessive resources. Three variants: 1) Manual lock by product group on specific query pattern 2) Automatic block after query failed to complete too many times 3) Automatic block due to resource consumption pattern detection
solution: 1) Optimize query following best practices at https://aka.ms/logqueryperf 2) For manual lock variant: open CRI with Draft product group to request release, but still must improve query 3) For automatic blocks: improve query efficiency - use TimeGenerated filter first, reduce data volume, use has instead of contains, avoid high cardinality joins
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Query execution/Common queries issues and how to handle them

symptom: Query returns partial or incomplete results (HTTP 200 with PartialError code) with data appearing missing or 0 results - query exceeded maximum allowed memory of 5GB
rootCause: Query consumed more memory than the 5GB per-iterator limit in Log Analytics (cannot be increased). Usually caused by inefficient join operators or summarize operations on large datasets. The response returns 200 OK but contains PartialError with incomplete data.
solution: 1) Check Draft telemetry for partialQueryFailures using requestId 2) Optimize query: reduce data volume before join/summarize, use TimeGenerated filter first, reduce cardinality 3) Note: maxmemoryconsumptionperiterator cannot be modified in Log Analytics unlike native ADX 4) Consider breaking query into smaller time ranges
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Query execution/Common queries issues and how to handle them

symptom: KQL query using join operator without explicit kind returns fewer results than expected - matching rows appear to be missing from the result set
rootCause: Default join flavor in KQL is innerunique (not inner), which deduplicates the left side of the join keeping only one row per key. This is by design but often unexpected by users who assume standard inner join semantics.
solution: Explicitly specify kind=inner in the join operator to get standard inner join behavior: Table1 | join kind=inner Table2 on Key. The innerunique flavor retains only one left-side record per key, which reduces result count.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Query execution/Common queries issues and how to handle them

symptom: Query using arg() cross-resource pattern from Azure Monitor Logs to Azure Resource Graph returns no data or empty results
rootCause: Customer may be hitting ARG integration limitations such as unsupported resource types, incorrect query syntax, scope restrictions, or resource types not yet available in ARG
solution: 1) Review ARG integration limitations documentation 2) Verify query syntax follows documented format 3) Ensure targeted resource types are supported by ARG 4) Check if required permissions are in place
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Query execution/Query Azure Resource Graph(ARG) from Azure Monitor

symptom: Query to Azure Resource Graph from Azure Monitor fails with permissions or authorization error when using arg() pattern
rootCause: User or service principal lacks required permissions for Azure Resource Graph data access. ARG requires separate RBAC permissions from Log Analytics workspace permissions.
solution: Verify required permissions are correctly set per ARG documentation. User needs Reader role on target subscriptions/resources for ARG queries. Check both Log Analytics workspace RBAC and ARG resource-level permissions.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Query execution/Query Azure Resource Graph(ARG) from Azure Monitor

symptom: Query to Azure Resource Graph from Azure Monitor fails with throttling error - query cannot be executed as it was throttled by the system to protect stability
rootCause: Customer exceeding Azure Resource Graph rate limiting thresholds. ARG has its own throttling limits separate from Log Analytics query throttling.
solution: 1) Collect proof of ARG throttling from Draft telemetry using failed requestId - innererror code shows AzureResourceGraphRateLimiting 2) Engage Azure Resource Graph CSS team with proof of throttling, Draft telemetry evidence, and business justification 3) Support boundary: ARG throttling issues owned by ARG team, not Log Analytics
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Query execution/Query Azure Resource Graph(ARG) from Azure Monitor

symptom: Query execution fails with HTTP 401 - Valid authentication was not provided or AuthorizationRequiredError when querying Log Analytics via API or SDK
rootCause: Authentication (AuthN) failure - AAD token validation failed. Common causes: no authentication provided in request, token expired, or token was issued for a different scope/service (e.g., using ARM token for Draft API)
solution: 1) Verify a valid AAD token is supplied with correct audience/scope for the API being called 2) For Draft API: use token scoped to api.loganalytics.io 3) For ARM API: use token scoped to management.azure.com 4) Check token expiration and refresh mechanism 5) If issue persists, engage Azure Active Directory team
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Query execution/Query execution issues due to AuthN or AuthZ failures

symptom: Query execution is slow or fails with HTTP 403 when user has custom RBAC role with per-table permissions instead of wildcard query permissions on Log Analytics workspace
rootCause: RBAC evaluation is slow because the system must traverse and check permissions for each individual table in the workspace when per-table permissions are defined in custom roles. Combined with large number of custom log tables, this causes significant AuthZ overhead.
solution: 1) Use wildcard permission microsoft.operationalinsights/workspaces/query/*/read instead of per-table permissions in custom roles 2) Reduce number of custom role assignments and role definitions in subscription 3) Reduce number of custom log tables if possible 4) If issue persists after optimization, escalate to DataPlane_API_QueryService via ICM
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Log Analytics/Troubleshooting Guides/Query execution/Query execution issues due to AuthN or AuthZ failures

symptom: Azure VM Resource ID is missing in Heartbeat table - VM shows as 'Unhealthy' in Azure Security Center
rootCause: Azure Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) at 169.254.169.254 is not accessible from the VM, typically due to a missing route for 169.254.169.254 or network/firewall blocking IMDS access. Also, old SCOM agents (SCOM 2016 and earlier) do not support Resource ID.
solution: 1) Query Heartbeat table for ResourceId field 2) Test IMDS access: PowerShell Invoke-RestMethod or curl to http://169.254.169.254/metadata/instance 3) If IMDS fails, check route table for 169.254.169.254 (route print / netstat -r) 4) Add route if missing: route add 169.254.169.254/32 <gateway-ip> metric 1 -p 5) If still failing, engage Azure VM team for IMDS issues or Windows Networking team for SSL/TLS issues 6) For SCOM: upgrade to SCOM 2019 or later
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Agent Configurations/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Azure VM Resource Id missing

symptom: VM keeps reconnecting to a Log Analytics Workspace after being disconnected - often reconnects to a DefaultWorkspace-{subscriptionId}-{region} workspace without user action
rootCause: Azure Policy or Microsoft Defender for Cloud auto-provisioning is configured to deploy monitoring agents, causing the VM to automatically reconnect to a workspace after manual disconnection
solution: 1) Check if Azure Policy is enforcing agent deployment to a workspace 2) Check if Microsoft Defender for Cloud auto-provisioning is enabled 3) Disable Defender for Cloud auto-provisioning if it is the cause - the VM will stop auto-reconnecting. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/defender-for-cloud/auto-deploy-azure-monitoring-agent
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Agent Configurations/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting VM keep reconnecting to Workspace after its disconnected from Workspace

symptom: Linux VM metrics configured in DCR Perf data source appear with different names in Azure Monitor Metric Explorer under azure.vm.linux.guestmetrics namespace - customer cannot find their configured metrics
rootCause: AMA Linux uses Telegraf internally with an intermediate.json mapping file that translates Perf counter names (used in DCR/workspace) to different metric display names in Metric Explorer. The mapping is not 1:1 with the counter names customers configure.
solution: Check the intermediate.json file at /var/lib/waagent/Microsoft.Azure.Monitor.AzureMonitorLinuxAgent-{version}/config/telegraf_configs/intermediate.json for the exact mapping between DCR counter names and Metric Explorer display names. Key mappings: mem.available_percent='% Available Memory', cpu.usage_active='% Processor Time', disk.free_percent='% Free Space', etc.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) for Linux/How-To/AMA Linux: HT:  How to match Linux metric names to counter names in DCR Perf data stream

symptom: In Linux network trace, cannot distinguish whether HIMDS (localhost:40342) calls originate from AMA or from other processes such as the ARC agent
rootCause: Both AMA and ARC agent use the HIMDS service on localhost:40342 for metadata and MSI token retrieval. Network trace shows identical TCP connections without obvious process-level differentiation.
solution: Inspect the User-Agent field in HTTP GET requests to localhost:40342. AMA uses 'cpprestsdk' as User-Agent (C++ REST SDK). Other processes like ARC agent use 'Boost.Beast' or different libraries. Filter network trace by User-Agent to isolate AMA-specific HIMDS calls.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) for Linux/How-To/How To: Distinguish ARC HIMDS calls from AMA on Linux HIMDS calls in a network trace

symptom: AMA agent on Linux not collecting data; mdsd.err log shows 'boost::filesystem::directory_iterator::construct: Permission denied' for /etc/opt/microsoft/azuremonitoragent/config-cache/configchunks
rootCause: Incorrect file system permissions on AMA config-cache directories. The configchunks directory (or parent directories) is not owned by syslog:syslog or lacks proper read/execute permissions, preventing the mdsd process from accessing DCR configuration files.
solution: Fix ownership: sudo chown -R syslog:syslog /etc/opt/microsoft/azuremonitoragent/config-cache/configchunks. Verify correct permissions: config-cache=710, azuremonitoragent=751, /var/opt/microsoft/azuremonitoragent/=751, /var/run/azuremonitoragent/=751, /opt/microsoft/azuremonitoragent/=751. All should be owned by syslog:syslog.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) for Linux/How-To/Troubleshooting AMA Agent data collection issues due to file system permissions

symptom: AMA agent cannot upload data to ODS endpoint; network trace shows TLS handshake fails with TCP Reset (TCP:Flags=R) after Client Hello during connection to *.ods.opinsights.azure.com:443
rootCause: Required TLS cipher suite TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 is disabled on the client machine. Azure endpoint cannot find a compatible cipher from the Client Hello cipher list, responds with TCP Reset to terminate the connection.
solution: Capture network trace (netsh trace start capture=yes, wait 5 min, netsh trace stop). Inspect TLS Client Hello for cipher suites. Ensure TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 is listed and enabled. Also verify TLS 1.2 is used and ServerName extension matches the ODS endpoint FQDN.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) for Windows/How-To/How to Analyze Network Trace/How to analyze AMA Network Trace ODS Connection

symptom: AMA agent fails to connect to ODS endpoint; TLS Server Hello shows certificate issued by non-Microsoft CA (e.g., firewall's own CA or internal enterprise CA) instead of expected Microsoft CA
rootCause: Network firewall or proxy is performing HTTPS/SSL/packet inspection, intercepting the TLS session and replacing the Microsoft CA certificate with its own. AMA detects this man-in-the-middle condition and blocks the connection to prevent data exfiltration.
solution: Configure the network firewall/proxy to bypass SSL inspection for Azure Monitor endpoints (*.ods.opinsights.azure.com:443). Verify by capturing network trace and checking the Issuer field in the TLS Server Hello Certificate — it must be a Microsoft CA. Common false indicator: connection appears to be reset by Azure but is actually reset by the inspecting firewall.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) for Windows/How-To/How to Analyze Network Trace/How to analyze AMA Network Trace ODS Connection

symptom: AMA Windows agent on Azure VM installed successfully but agent processes not running. No DCR downloaded. ExtensionHealth log shows 'QueryIMDSEndpoint ErrorCode:1601 ERROR: IMDS appears to not be present on this machine. Error: 12002; Message: The operation timed out'
rootCause: IMDS endpoint (169.254.169.254) is unreachable from the VM due to missing network route in the routing table
solution: 1) Test IMDS connectivity: Invoke-RestMethod -Headers @{Metadata=true} -Method GET -Uri 'http://169.254.169.254/metadata/instance?api-version=2021-02-01' 2) Check route table: 'route print' for 169.254.169.254 entry 3) If route missing, add it: route add 169.254.169.254/32 <default-gateway-IP> metric 1 -p 4) If still failing, consult Azure VM Team via collaboration
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) for Windows/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting AMA Windows - ERROR: IMDS appears to not be present on this machine

symptom: Azure VM or Arc server extension auto-upgrade (enableAutomaticUpgrade=true) keeps failing or rolling back, causing continuous retry and high disk consumption
rootCause: Latest extension version has an issue causing upgrade failure, triggering automatic rollback loop with repeated retry attempts consuming disk space
solution: 1) Check extension upgrade logs (Arc: gc_ext.log, Azure VM: CommandExecution.log under C:\WindowsAzure\Logs\Plugins) 2) Query RSM Kusto VMAutoExtensionUpgradeEvent/ArcVMAutoExtensionUpgradeEvent for upgrade status 3) Disable automatic extension upgrade to pause retry loop 4) Downgrade to last known good version via Set-AzVMExtension 5) Re-enable auto-upgrade once issue is fixed in newer version
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) for Windows/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting extension auto upgrade issue on windows

symptom: AMA agent on Windows is not sending heartbeats to Log Analytics Workspace. Errors in maeventtable.csv: 'SSL Error: WINHTTP_CALLBACK_STATUS_FLAG_CERT_REV_FAILED' and 'WINHTTP_CALLBACK_STATUS_FLAG_INVALID_CA SSL invalid CA'. curl.exe to global.handler.control.monitor.azure.com returns 'SEC_E_UNTRUSTED_ROOT (0x80090325)'. System Event ID 36882: certificate received from remote server was issued by untrusted certificate authority.
rootCause: Group Policy 'Turn off Automatic Root Certificates Update' is enabled (registry key HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\SystemCertificates\AuthRoot\DisableRootAutoUpdate = 1), preventing automatic download of trusted root certificates. The machine's trusted root certificate store is missing required Microsoft/Azure CA certificates needed for TLS validation to Azure Monitor endpoints.
solution: 1) Check registry key HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\SystemCertificates\AuthRoot\DisableRootAutoUpdate - if value is 1, set to 0 to enable automatic root cert updates. 2) Run 'certutil -verifystore root' to verify certificate store. 3) Customer should review Group Policy settings with their AD Services team. 4) If issue persists after enabling auto root cert update, engage AD team to enable CAPI2 logging for deeper certificate chain analysis.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) for Windows/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting AMA Agent is not sending heartbeats with errors CERT_E_UNTRUSTEDROOT, WINHTTP_CALLBACK_STATUS_FLAG_CERT_REV_FAILED

symptom: AMA agent on Windows not sending heartbeats with SSL errors WINHTTP_CALLBACK_STATUS_FLAG_CERT_REV_FAILED even though trusted root certificates are present. TLS connection to Azure Monitor endpoints fails during certificate revocation check.
rootCause: Machine is unable to reach Certificate Revocation List (CRL) endpoints required for TLS certificate validation. Firewall or proxy is blocking outbound HTTPS/HTTP access to Microsoft CRL distribution points.
solution: Verify network connectivity to CRL endpoints documented at https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/security/fundamentals/azure-ca-details. Ensure firewall/proxy allows access to CRL URLs (e.g., crl.microsoft.com, mscrl.microsoft.com, ocsp.msocsp.com). If behind a firewall, add the CRL URLs to the allowlist.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) for Windows/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting AMA Agent is not sending heartbeats with errors CERT_E_UNTRUSTEDROOT, WINHTTP_CALLBACK_STATUS_FLAG_CERT_REV_FAILED

symptom: OMS Agent for Linux fails to install or connect when using OpenSSL 1.1.0 on non-x86_64 (32-bit) platforms, or when the system has OpenSSL versions earlier than 1.x installed.
rootCause: OpenSSL 1.1.0 is only supported on x86_x64 platforms (64-bit). OpenSSL versions earlier than 1.x are not supported on any platform by the OMS Linux Agent.
solution: Verify the Linux system uses a supported OpenSSL version (1.x or later). If using OpenSSL 1.1.0, ensure the platform architecture is x86_64. Upgrade OpenSSL if the version is older than 1.x.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Common Concepts/Linux Agent Basics

symptom: OMS Linux Agent fails to connect through a proxy server that does not require authentication. No clear error message is shown but connectivity to workspace fails.
rootCause: The Linux agent implementation requires proxy credentials to be configured even when the proxy server itself does not require authentication.
solution: Configure a pseudo/dummy username and password (any arbitrary values) in the proxy configuration for the Linux agent, even if the proxy does not require authentication.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Common Concepts/Linux Agent Basics

symptom: OMS Linux Agent proxy connection fails with a proxy connection error when the proxy password contains special characters such as '@'.
rootCause: Special characters in the proxy password (e.g., '@') are parsed incorrectly by the agent's proxy configuration parser, causing the connection URL to be malformed.
solution: URL-encode the proxy password before configuring it. For example, encode '@' as '%40'. Use a URL encoding tool such as https://www.urldecoder.org/ to encode the password string.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Common Concepts/Linux Agent Basics

symptom: Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) on hardened Linux systems fails to function properly - agent service may not start, not collect data, or crash. Syslog user lacks required directory permissions.
rootCause: Hardened Linux systems restrict default directory permissions. AMA requires the syslog user to have specific read/write/execute permissions on multiple AMA directories that hardening policies may lock down.
solution: Set required directory permissions: /etc/opt/microsoft/azuremonitoragent (751, owner: root), /etc/opt/microsoft/azuremonitoragent/config-cache (710, owner: syslog), /opt/microsoft/azuremonitoragent (751, root), /run/azuremonitoragent (751, syslog), /var/opt/microsoft/azuremonitoragent (751, syslog), /var/lib/waagent/Microsoft.Azure.Monitor.AzureMonitorLinuxAgent-<version> (751, root), /var/log/azure/Microsoft.Azure.Monitor.AzureMonitorLinuxAgent/ (751, root). Ensure syslog user and group exist on the system.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Common Concepts/Proper Directory Permissions needed for Syslog User for AMA on Linux

symptom: Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) or Windows Telemetry client fails to upload data to Log Analytics workspace. CAPI2 event logs show certificate chain validation errors (e.g., error code 0x80092012). Network trace shows TLS Server Hello from internal IP with non-Microsoft certificate issuer.
rootCause: Customer's proxy or firewall is performing SSL/HTTPS inspection, intercepting Microsoft SSL certificates and re-signing them with the proxy's own CA. This breaks the certificate trust chain required by MMA and Windows Telemetry.
solution: Configure the proxy/firewall to bypass SSL inspection for all Microsoft monitoring endpoints: *.ods.opinsights.azure.com, *.oms.opinsights.azure.com, *.blob.core.windows.net, *.azure-automation.net, and *.events.data.microsoft.com. Verify the fix by enabling CAPI2 logging (Event Viewer > CAPI2 Operational, filter for Event IDs 10-11,30,50-53,90) or capturing a Wireshark trace filtered on 'tls.handshake.certificate contains microsoft' to confirm the certificate issuer is Microsoft.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Common Concepts/Network Tracing for MMA and Upgrade Readiness

symptom: Azure Support Center (ASC) shows error when trying to find Data Collection Rules (DCRs) associated with a VM when the DCR is in a different subscription than the VM. The dataCollectionRules button under Microsoft.Insights in ASC fails to list cross-subscription DCR associations.
rootCause: ASC's built-in DCR lookup under Microsoft.Insights service provider only works within the same subscription as the VM. It cannot discover DCR associations that exist in a foreign (different) subscription.
solution: Use Azure Resource Graph (ARG) query in ASC: Navigate to the VM's subscription in ASC > click ARG Query Editor tab > run the insightsresources query filtering by type 'microsoft.insights/datacollectionruleassociations', the VM's resource group, subscription ID, and VM name. This will output all DCR associations including those in foreign subscriptions.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Data Collection Rules (DCR)/How-To/HT: Find out the DCRs associated with a VM in foreign subscription

symptom: VMInsight DCR performance counters stop collecting data after customer modified the DCR performance counter configuration. Inspecting the DCR JSON reveals the stream name under performanceCounters dataSource has been changed from the original 'Microsoft-InsightsMetrics' to a different/incorrect value.
rootCause: Customer manually modified the VMInsight-created DCR's performance counter stream name, breaking the data flow. While the counter section still points to the correct perf table, the stream name no longer matches the dataflow destination configuration.
solution: 1) Export the DCR template from Azure portal (Automation > Export Template) 2) Download template and parameters files 3) Edit the template JSON: locate the performanceCounters dataSource and fix the invalid stream name to match the valid stream name from the dataFlows node (should be 'Microsoft-InsightsMetrics') 4) In Azure portal, go to Custom Template Deployment > Build Your Own Template > Load the modified template file 5) Load the parameters file 6) Deploy to overwrite the existing DCR with the corrected configuration.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Data Collection Rules (DCR)/How-To/How To: Restore your VMInsight DCR Performance Counter Stream

symptom: Second Data Collection Rule (DCR) association replaces the first when two DCRs with the same name (but in different resource groups) are both associated to the same VM. After associating DCR2 to the VM, checking DCR1's Resources shows the VM is missing.
rootCause: Expected behavior (by design). The Azure portal UX defaults the association name to '{VMResourceID}/providers/Microsoft.Insights/dataCollectionRuleAssociations/{DCRName}-association'. When two DCRs share the same name, they generate identical association names, causing the second PUT to overwrite the first association.
solution: This is a known platform limitation, not a bug. Workarounds: 1) Rename one of the DCRs to ensure unique names 2) If using REST API, explicitly specify unique association names (e.g., 'test1' vs 'test2') to avoid collision. The portal UX does not currently support custom association naming.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Data Collection Rules (DCR)/Known Issues/DCR: Limitation: DCRs with same names replace each other

symptom: MMA (Microsoft Monitoring Agent) Windows agent loses data or shows gaps in Log Analytics workspace after a network connectivity interruption or outage
rootCause: MMA Windows agent buffers data locally with a default max of 100MB (configurable up to 1.5GB via registry). Agent retries exponentially over 10 attempts up to a max interval of 8.5 hours. Once the buffer is full, oldest data is discarded. Retry timing is slightly randomized to prevent thundering herd.
solution: 1) Restore network connectivity to Log Analytics workspace 2) Increase buffer size via registry if data volume is high (default 100MB, max 1.5GB) - refer to 'How to modify MMA Caching, Rate Limits, Tuning Parameters' wiki 3) Agent will automatically resume uploading buffered data once connectivity is restored 4) After outage, agent processes backlog until caught up to latest data
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Common Concepts/Windows Agent Basics (Microsoft Monitoring Agent)

symptom: Deploying Container Insights DCR with ingestion time transformations (KQL transform) fails with InvalidPayload error: Data flows cannot reference stream id individually since it is being configured through stream group and not individually in data sources
rootCause: Container Insights DCRs use StreamGroups feature which groups multiple data sources into a single dataflow configuration. StreamGroups do not support individual stream-level KQL transforms because the process cannot determine which data for which output stream needs transformation.
solution: 1) Remove the KQL transform from the DCR if stream groups are in place (recommended). 2) Workaround (not recommended): explicitly define individual stream declarations and dataflows for each data type instead of using stream groups, then add KQL transform to the specific stream you wish to modify. Note: dynamic stream group configurations are agent version specific.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Data Collection Rules (DCR)/Known Issues/Draft - Known Issue - Invalid Payload error when deploying Container Insights DCR with transform KQL criteria

symptom: Windows performance counters not uploading to Log Analytics workspace via MMA or AMA agent, while other machines report the same counters successfully
rootCause: The perf counter is not producing values locally on the affected machine (corrupt counter, missing application counter, or counter name mismatch in DCR configuration)
solution: 1) Validate counter locally using Perfmon (add counter and check values) 2) Validate using typeperf command: 'typeperf -qx | FIND /i "<counter>"' to find exact path, then 'typeperf -sc 5 <counter>' to verify values 3) If counter not producing values, engage respective counter owner team (SQL team for SQL counters, Windows support for OS counters, 3rd party for app counters) 4) For custom DCR counters, copy exact counter path from typeperf output to avoid non-binding space characters
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/How-To/General/How to check Windows Perf counter is working on local Machine

symptom: Customer observes an unknown/unrecognized workspace ID configured on the Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) that does not belong to any Log Analytics workspace in their Azure subscription
rootCause: The workspace was auto-provisioned by Azure Policy, Defender for Cloud auto-provisioning, or Microsoft Defender for Endpoint (MDE) which configures MMA to report to Microsoft's internal subscription workspace
solution: 1) Query workspace details using Azure Data Explorer (oibeftprd.kusto.windows.net/AMSTelemetry) to find the subscription ID 2) Check if customer has access to that subscription 3) If accessible: check for manual LAW addition, Azure Policy auto-adding LAW, or Defender for Cloud auto-provisioning under Environment Settings 4) If subscription is outside tenant: check Microsoft 365 Defender Portal (sip.security.microsoft.com) > Settings > Endpoints > Device Management > Onboarding for the workspace ID to confirm MDE involvement 5) Guide customer on MDE onboard/offboard procedures
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/How-To/General/How to find details about an unknown Workspace on MMA Agent

symptom: Need to downgrade Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) extension to a previous version due to defect in the upgraded version, but Azure portal VM extension blade does not support reverting to previous versions
rootCause: Azure portal UI does not provide a built-in option to install a specific older version of VM extensions; product group identified a defect in the current extension version and recommends downgrading
solution: 1) Uninstall current extension from VM > Extensions blade 2) Wait 2 minutes 3) Open Azure PowerShell and run: For Windows: Set-AzVMExtension -Name AzureMonitorWindowsAgent -ExtensionType AzureMonitorWindowsAgent -Publisher Microsoft.Azure.Monitor -ResourceGroupName <RG> -VMName <VM> -Location <region> -TypeHandlerVersion <major.minor> -DisableAutoUpgradeMinorVersion -EnableAutomaticUpgrade $false | For Linux: Set-AzVMExtension -Name AzureMonitorLinuxAgent -ExtensionType AzureMonitorLinuxAgent -Publisher Microsoft.Azure.Monitor -ResourceGroupName <RG> -VMName <VM> -Location <region> -TypeHandlerVersion <major.minor> -DisableAutoUpgradeMinorVersion -EnableAutomaticUpgrade $false | Note: use major.minor version only (e.g. 1.16 not 1.16.0), must include both -DisableAutoUpgradeMinorVersion and -EnableAutomaticUpgrade $false switches
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/How-To/How To force install previous version of agent extension via Azure PowerShell

symptom: Linux agent (OMS/AMA) logs or OpenSSL/curl command output shows error "unable to get local issuer certificate" when connecting to Azure Monitor endpoints
rootCause: SSL certificate chain on the Azure Monitor endpoint is not fully trusted by the Linux system - one or more intermediate/root CA certificates (e.g., DigiCert, Microsoft Azure RSA TLS Issuing CA) are missing from the VM's trusted certificate store. Common on custom images or on-premise VMs not sourced from Azure Marketplace.
solution: 1) Identify which certificate in the chain is missing by examining the openssl s_client output 2) Download the missing certificate(s) from https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/security/fundamentals/azure-ca-details 3) Import the certificate into the VM's trusted certificate store using distro-specific methods (RHEL: update-ca-trust; Ubuntu: update-ca-certificates) 4) Re-test with openssl s_client to confirm full chain is now trusted
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/How-To/How To: Network Analysis/Linux Agent Logs, OpenSSL or curl error: "unable to get local issuer certificate"

symptom: OpenSSL s_client command to Azure Monitor endpoints (e.g., global.handler.control.monitor.azure.com:443) reports "no peer certificate available" - TLS handshake fails with no server certificate received
rootCause: Firewall or network security device is blocking the TLS connection - the Client Hello request goes unanswered, or the firewall (acting as man-in-the-middle) injects a TCP Reset or TCP FIN into the stream, preventing the Server Hello packet from reaching the client. The server certificate is never delivered.
solution: 1) Verify with network trace that Client Hello is sent but Server Hello is not received 2) Check if a firewall/proxy/NSG is intercepting traffic to Azure Monitor endpoints on port 443 3) Add firewall rules to allow outbound connectivity to required Azure Monitor endpoints (*.monitor.azure.com, *.ods.opinsights.azure.com, etc.) 4) If firewall injects TCP Reset/FIN, work with network team to identify and whitelist the connection 5) Re-test with openssl s_client after firewall rule changes
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/How-To/How To: Network Analysis/OpenSSL reports "no peer certificate available"
Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Known Issues/Private Link - ODS fix
symptom: AMA data ingestion stops after Private Link ODS fix enforcement. Public traffic through AMA->DCE/DCR->ODS->GIG path fails when LA Workspace and DCE have network isolation set to reject public traffic but no AMPLS is configured.
rootCause: ODS fix (rolled out April 2023) enforces strict Private Link validation for AMA data flow. If LA Workspace and DCE network isolation is set to 'No' (accept only private network traffic) without proper AMPLS mapping, all public ingestion traffic is rejected.
solution: Check Network Isolation settings for LA Workspace and DCE. Either set 'Accept data ingestion from public networks' to 'Yes' if public ingestion is needed, or configure Azure Monitor Private Link Scope (AMPLS) and map it to both LA Workspace and DCE to enable private network ingestion path.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Linux Azure Diagnostics (LAD)/How-To/How To check if the watchdog service is preventing the installation of LAD
symptom: LAD extension installation fails on Linux VM. Extension log (/var/log/azure/Microsoft.Azure.Diagnostics.LinuxDiagnostic/extension.log) shows: 'Failed to restart rsyslog.service: Operation refused, unit rsyslog.service may be requested by dependency only (it is configured to refuse manual start/stop)'.
rootCause: The watchdog service configuration file (/etc/systemd/system/rsyslog.service.d/watchdog.conf) has RefuseManualStop=true, which prevents rsyslog from being restarted. LAD installation requires restarting the syslog service.
solution: Edit /etc/systemd/system/rsyslog.service.d/watchdog.conf and change RefuseManualStop=true to RefuseManualStop=false. Restart the watchdog service: 'sudo systemctl restart watchdog.service'. Then reinstall the LAD extension.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Linux Azure Diagnostics (LAD)/Known Issues/Performance data collection failed via AMA if LAD is enabled
symptom: Performance data (metrics) missing in Azure Monitor when both AMA and LAD extensions are enabled on a Linux VM. After VM restart, metrics may work temporarily but stop again after a while.
rootCause: AMA and LAD share a common telegraf setup for metrics collection. This coexistence is not supported — LAD occupies the telegraf service, blocking AMA from collecting performance data. Even after VM restart, LAD eventually reclaims telegraf.
solution: Remove the LAD extension and restart AMA agent: 'systemctl restart azuremonitoragent'. Do not run AMA and LAD simultaneously on the same VM for performance/metrics collection.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Linux Azure Diagnostics (LAD)/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Unable to deploy LAD in RED HAT 8.6+ with error "Unable to find a match: policycoreutils-python"
symptom: LAD extension installation fails on Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8.6+ with error in extension log: 'No match for argument: policycoreutils-python' / 'Error: Unable to find a match: policycoreutils-python'. LAD defaults to version 3.0 when installed from portal.
rootCause: LAD version 3.0 (default via portal/CLI) depends on the policycoreutils-python package which was removed/renamed in RHEL 8.6+. The package is no longer available in the RHEL 8.6+ repositories.
solution: Remove LAD 3.0 from portal. Install Python 2 ('yum install -y python2'), set python2 as default ('sudo update-alternatives --install /usr/bin/python python /usr/bin/python2 1'). Then install LAD 4.0 using Set-AzVMExtension with -TypeHandlerVersion 4.0 and proper storage/identity configuration.

symptom: MMA extension uninstall fails - HealthService remains in partial deletion state after uninstall attempt. Service status shows error in services.msc, MOMagent_install.log shows Service could not be installed with error hr=80070005 at RemoveDirectoryAndChildren
rootCause: HealthService is stuck in a partial deletion state after a failed or interrupted uninstallation attempt, preventing proper service removal and blocking new agent installation
solution: Delete HealthService manually: 1) Without reboot: open elevated cmd, run sc.exe delete HealthService 2) With reboot: remove registry HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\HealthService 3) Remove agent folder C:\Program Files\Microsoft Monitoring Agent if it still exists 4) Remove extension from C:\Packages\Plugins\Microsoft.EnterpriseCloud.Monitoring.MicrosoftMonitoringAgent 5) Remove certificate: Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\Microsoft Monitoring Agent | Remove-Item
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) for Windows/How-To/How to Purge and force uninstall MMA

symptom: Cannot install or uninstall MMA agent due to partial/corrupted previous installation - agent processes not running, agent directory C:\Program Files\Microsoft Monitoring Agent\Agent is empty, but Get-WmiObject Win32_Product still shows Microsoft Monitoring Agent installed
rootCause: Previous MMA version left partial/corrupted installation artifacts in Windows Installer registry while actual agent files were removed or corrupted, creating an inconsistent state that blocks newer version installation
solution: 1) Run Get-WmiObject -Class Win32_Product -Filter Name=Microsoft Monitoring Agent to get IdentifyingNumber GUID 2) Match GUID to MMA version using known version table in MMAVMExtension source 3) Download matching MSI version 4) Double-click MSI to install (re-creates expected state) 5) Double-click MSI again to uninstall via Remove option 6) Verify: Get-WmiObject should return no results 7) If still failing, use Microsoft Fix Programs tool (support.microsoft.com Fix problems that block programs from being installed or removed)
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) for Windows/How-To/How to Purge and force uninstall MMA

symptom: Cloned Windows VMs all have the same SourceComputerId (Agent ID) for Microsoft Monitoring Agent, causing Azure Automation Update Management and Azure Security Center to not distinguish between machines
rootCause: When a VM is cloned from a golden/master image that had MMA installed and configured, the SourceComputerId is preserved in the clone, resulting in all cloned machines sharing the same agent identity
solution: 1) Stop HealthService 2) Rename 'Health Service State' folder 3) Rename 'AzureAutomation' folder 4) Delete MMA registry key under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Microsoft Operations Manager\3.0\Agent Management Groups 5) Start HealthService - this forces regeneration of a new SourceComputerId. If agent is part of Azure Automation Hybrid Worker group, re-register manually after regeneration
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) for Windows/How-To/How to regenerate the SourceComputerId (Agent ID) on the Windows Agent

symptom: Events from Microsoft-Windows-DNSServer Analytic channel are not collected by MMA agent despite being enabled on the workspace. Operations Manager event log shows error: 'Windows Event Log Provider is still unable to open the Microsoft-Windows-DNSServer/Analytical event log' with error 'The requested operation cannot be performed over an enabled direct channel'
rootCause: MMA agent does not support collecting events from Analytic or Debug channels in Event Viewer (e.g., channels enabled via wevtutil.exe set-log). Only standard operational event logs are supported for collection
solution: Currently there is no workaround to collect events from Analytic/Debug channels via MMA. Inform customer this is an unsupported scenario. Consider alternative collection methods such as Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) with custom XPath queries or forwarding logs to a standard event log first
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) for Windows/Known Issues/Events from Microsoft-Windows-DNSServer | Analytic Logs are not collected

symptom: MMA agent fails to apply configuration with HealthService Event ID 7022 error 'Keyset does not exist' (0x80090016) and Service Connector Event ID 3009 'Loading the private key for the client authentication certificate failed' on a cloned Windows server
rootCause: The server was cloned from a golden/master image that had MMA agent installed AND configured (connected to a Log Analytics workspace). The cloned server retains certificates with the original server name, causing private key mismatch errors when trying to authenticate
solution: 1) Stop HealthService (Stop-Service HealthService -Force) 2) Rename 'C:\Program Files\Microsoft Monitoring Agent\Agent\Health Service State' folder to '.old' 3) Start HealthService (Start-Service HealthService). This forces the agent to regenerate certificates and configuration for the current machine identity
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) for Windows/Known Issues/MMA agent is not able to apply the configuration if the server was cloned

symptom: Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) for SCOM exhibits high handle count after applying System Center Operations Manager 2019 Update Rollup 3 (UR3), especially observed on Domain Controllers or machines with security event log collection enabled
rootCause: Code defect in SCOM 2019 UR3 (Bug 879292) causes the MMA agent to accumulate handles excessively when processing security event logs
solution: If the agent is SCOM code tree: transfer incident to System Center Operations Manager Support team. If the agent is Log Analytics version: follow MMA high memory/CPU troubleshooting guides. Verify agent version against Log Analytics agent version list to determine code tree
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) for Windows/Known Issues/Microsoft Monitoring Agent for SCOM High Handle Count after Update Rollup 3 is applied

symptom: All data types suddenly stop ingesting to the Log Analytics Workspace and Application Insights after customer disables public access for ingestion in network isolation settings without having an Azure Monitor Private Link Scope (AMPLS) configured
rootCause: Customer disables public access for ingestion under network isolation settings thinking it adds extra security, but without an AMPLS configured this effectively blocks ALL ingestion from all machines in all networks. This is a common user misconfiguration
solution: Re-enable public access for ingestion in the workspace/AI component network isolation settings. Educate customer that disabling public access is specifically for Azure Monitor Private Link scenarios (requires AMPLS). After re-enabling, allow up to 30+ minutes for the configuration to propagate and for machines to resume sending data. Reference: https://docs.microsoft.com/azure/azure-monitor/logs/private-link-security#manage-access-from-outside-of-private-links-scopes
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) for Windows/Known Issues/Network Isolation Settings Misconfiguration
Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) for Windows/Known Issues/Sporadic agent delay in the upload of custom and IIS logs
symptom: MMA (Microsoft Monitoring Agent) has sporadic delay uploading custom logs and IIS logs to Log Analytics workspace. Latency appears and disappears intermittently but no data loss occurs.
rootCause: Bug in the Management Pack for custom log uploads. The agent reports upload errors on the same channel used for blob upload notifications. ODS NotifyBlobUpload endpoint rejects these with HTTP 400, causing the agent to retry endlessly until restart or queue overflow.
solution: Restart the MMA agent to temporarily clear the stuck queue. For recurring cases, use a script to auto-restart the agent when Event ID 8000 (source: HealthService) appears in Operations Manager event log. A permanent fix was deployed (IcM 182588463, July 2020).

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) for Windows/Known Issues/The Agent had an unknown failure 12044L
symptom: MMA agent logs Event ID 3009 (loading private key for client auth certificate failed, error 0x800706D9) and Event ID 4004 (HTTP operation failed with error 12044L). No data received from agent. TestCloudConnection.exe passes.
rootCause: The Windows CNG Key Isolation service is stopped on the agent machine, preventing MMA from accessing certificates via LSASS.
solution: Start the CNG Key Isolation service. Investigate why it stopped with Windows platform or Directory Services support.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) for Windows/Known Issues/The Agent had an unknown failure 2114
symptom: MMA agent logs Event ID 4004: HTTP operation failed with error 'The Server service is not started' (2114L). No data received from agent. TestCloudConnection.exe passes.
rootCause: The Windows Server service (LanmanServer) is stopped on the agent machine.
solution: Start the Windows Server service (LanmanServer). Investigate why it stopped with Windows platform support. The service exists on both server and client OS.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) for Windows/Known Issues/The MMA cannot be installed on a Computer running Roles Like SCOM Management Server, Gateway, Service Manager etc
symptom: MMA installation fails with MSI error Event ID 1013: 'The Microsoft Monitoring Agent cannot be installed on a computer on which the Operations Manager management server, gateway server, Operations console, operational database, web console, System Center Essentials, or System Center Service Manager is already installed.'
rootCause: SCOM/Service Manager roles include a native HealthService version that cannot be upgraded by the standalone MMA installer. The MMA installer detects the existing SCOM component and blocks installation (by design).
solution: This is expected behavior by design. Customer should use SCOM 2016 or 2019 with latest Update Rollup, which supports directly connecting to Log Analytics workspace as a Direct Agent scenario.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) for Windows/Known Issues/The agent configurations could not be saved
symptom: MMA agent configurations cannot be saved when updated via the Control Panel applet.
rootCause: Default permissions for built-in Administrators group changed from Full Control to Read on registry key HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\services\HealthService\Parameters.
solution: Take a registry backup. Restore default permissions (Full Control for System and Administrators) on HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\services\HealthService\Parameters. Ensure inheritance applies to child objects.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Monitor Agents/Agents/Microsoft Monitoring Agent (MMA) for Windows/Known Issues/Updated IIS application pool crashed in SCOM 1801 with latest MMA version
symptom: Using MMA downloaded from Azure portal in a SCOM 1801 environment causes IIS application pool crashes.
